museum.name: Gaming Time Machine
museum.dek: December is astonishing: Mega Man, Final Fantasy and Phantasy Star arrive within days.
museum.curatorNote: December has three major Japanese release anchors, plus UK Christmas context.

sections.title: News
sections.subtitle: Five researched moments, with broad context separated from confident month-level claims.

items.title: Mega Man is released in Japan
items.body: Capcom introduces Robot Masters, stage select and a hero that will become far larger later.

items.title: Final Fantasy is released in Japan
items.body: Square's RPG begins a series that will reshape console RPG expectations.

items.title: Phantasy Star arrives on Sega Master System in Japan
items.body: Sega answers the RPG boom with science fantasy, first-person dungeons and impressive presentation.

items.title: Japan's RPG race is vivid
items.body: Dragon Quest, Final Fantasy, Phantasy Star and Zelda II show console games becoming deeper and longer.

items.title: British Christmas remains micro-led
items.body: For many UK players, these Japanese landmarks are not yet the games under the tree.

sections.title: Releases
sections.subtitle: Eight notable games from the year, led by month-specific anchors where the evidence supports them.

items.title: Mega Man
items.body: Capcom introduces stage select, Robot Masters and a future icon.

items.title: Final Fantasy
items.body: Square's RPG begins a series that will later define console role-playing worldwide.

items.title: Phantasy Star
items.body: Sega's science-fantasy RPG shows the Master System could host epic worlds.

items.title: Zelda II: The Adventure of Link
items.body: Nintendo's sequel moves Link into side-scrolling action and RPG-like progression.
items.sourceNote: Japanese FDS date is widely cited as January 14, 1987.

items.title: Dragon Quest II
items.body: Enix expands console RPG structure into a larger party and broader quest.

items.title: Contra
items.body: Konami's run-and-gun action begins in arcades before becoming a home-console memory.
items.sourceNote: MobyGames cites February 20, 1987 Japan arcade release.

items.title: Double Dragon
items.body: Technos turns street fighting into a two-player scrolling beat-'em-up template.
items.sourceNote: 1987 is secure; April timing appears in several summaries but is marked cautiously.

items.title: Leisure Suit Larry
items.body: Sierra's adult comedy adventure becomes a strange, talk-about-it computer-shop object.

sections.title: Hardware
sections.subtitle: Four machines or technology contexts that explain the month's place in gaming history.

items.title: PC Engine
items.body: NEC and Hudson's tiny HuCard console launches in Japan on October 30, opening a new hardware front.

items.title: NES / Famicom
items.body: The Japanese Famicom and Western NES histories are no longer moving in sync.
items.sourceNote: Used for Zelda II, Mega Man and regional caveats.

items.title: Sega Master System / Mark III
items.body: Sega's 8-bit console hosts Phantasy Star in Japan and remains a different proposition by region.

items.title: UK 8/16-bit computers
items.body: Spectrum, C64, CPC, Amiga and ST define much of the British lived experience.

sections.title: Online Life
sections.subtitle: Before online gaming was ordinary, paper, shops and local conversations carried the culture.

items.title: Print is the real network
items.body: ACE, The Games Machine, Crash and Zzap!64 move information faster than most players' modems.

items.title: BBS and online services remain specialist
items.body: A small enthusiast world exists, but ordinary gaming culture is still local and paper-led.

items.title: Import knowledge travels slowly
items.body: Japanese console releases become rumours, screenshots and tiny news items before they become playable.

items.title: The playground is still the feed
items.body: Cheats, arcade sightings and which conversion is terrible move by voice first.

sections.title: What It Felt Like
sections.subtitle: A short atmospheric reading of the month as a player might have met it.

items.title: The map was widening
items.body: December 1987 could mean a Japanese console RPG, a Sega arcade cabinet, a UK Spectrum review and a PC adventure all at once.

items.title: Britain felt format-first
items.body: The question was not just what game mattered, but which machine, which conversion and which magazine said so.

items.title: Arcades still had physical authority
items.body: R-Type, After Burner, Street Fighter and Shinobi felt like objects as much as software.

items.title: The future arrived out of order
items.body: Japanese consoles, American PCs and British micros made history in overlapping but unsynchronised timelines.

editorNotes: Initial AI-generated research draft for December 1987.
Month-specific dates are used only where the reviewed sources support them; uncertain items are marked in sourceNote/certainty fields.
Magazine cover images are reconstructed placeholders and should be replaced with verified/licensed scans if used editorially.
